annotations.label: Invertible matrix

anchor.snippet: an invertible matrix ( non-singular , non-degenerate or regular ) is a square matrix that has an inverse

annotations.note: Mathlib's general `Invertible` class applied to `Matrix n n α` formalizes a matrix having a two-sided inverse.

annotations.label: Invertible matrix (formal)

anchor.snippet: An n -by- n square matrix A is called invertible if there exists an n -by- n square matrix B such that

annotations.note: `Invertible A` packages a `⅟A` with `⅟A * A = 1` and `A * ⅟A = 1`, exactly the Wikipedia formal definition.

anchors.snippet: An n -by- n square matrix A is called invertible if there exists an n -by- n square matrix B such that

annotations.label: Matrix inversion

anchor.snippet: Matrix inversion is the process of finding the matrix which when multiplied by the original matrix gives the identity matrix

annotations.note: The noncomputable `Inv (Matrix n n α)` instance with `Matrix.inv_def : A⁻¹ = A.det⁻¹ʳ • A.adjugate` formalizes matrix inversion.

anchors.snippet: Matrix inversion is the process of finding the matrix which when multiplied by the original matrix gives the identity matrix

annotations.label: Gaussian elimination for inverse

anchor.snippet: Gaussian elimination is a useful and easy way to compute the inverse of a matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Gauss–Jordan/row-reduction algorithm formalized for computing matrix inverses.

annotations.label: Newton's method for inverse

anchor.snippet: A generalization of Newton's method as used for a multiplicative inverse algorithm may be convenient if it is convenient to find a suitable starting seed

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Dynamics.Newton` for scalar Newton iteration but no matrix-inverse Newton iteration.

annotations.label: Cayley–Hamilton inverse formula

anchor.snippet: The Cayley–Hamilton theorem allows the inverse of A to be expressed in terms of det( A ) , traces and powers of A

annotations.note: Cayley–Hamilton is formalized but the explicit inverse formula in terms of traces and powers is not stated.

annotations.label: Inverse via eigendecomposition

anchor.snippet: If matrix A can be eigendecomposed, and if none of its eigenvalues are zero, then A is invertible and its inverse is given by

annotations.note: No lemma in Mathlib expresses the inverse of a diagonalizable matrix via its eigendecomposition.

annotations.label: Inverse via Cholesky decomposition

anchor.snippet: If matrix A is positive definite , then its inverse can be obtained as

annotations.note: Cholesky decomposition is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjugate/cofactor inverse formula

anchor.snippet: Writing the transpose of the matrix of cofactors , known as an adjugate matrix , may also be an efficient way to calculate the inverse of small matrices

annotations.note: `Matrix.inv_def` (and `Matrix.nonsing_inv_apply`) states `A⁻¹ = A.det⁻¹ʳ • A.adjugate`, the adjugate formula for the inverse.

annotations.label: Inversion of 2x2 matrices

anchor.snippet: The cofactor equation listed above yields the following result for 2 × 2 matrices

annotations.note: `adjugate_fin_two_of` gives the explicit 2×2 adjugate; combined with `inv_def` it yields the 2×2 inverse, though no single packaged `Matrix.inv_fin_two` lemma exists.

annotations.label: Inversion of 3x3 matrices

anchor.snippet: A computationally efficient 3 × 3 matrix inversion is given by

annotations.note: Mathlib has the explicit 3×3 adjugate (`adjugate_fin_three_of`) which combined with `inv_def` gives the inverse, but no dedicated explicit-inverse lemma.

annotations.label: 3x3 inverse via cross/triple product

anchor.snippet: The general 3 × 3 inverse can be expressed concisely in terms of the cross product and triple product

annotations.note: No formulation of the 3×3 matrix inverse in terms of cross and triple products is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inversion of 4x4 matrices

anchor.snippet: For n = 4 , the Cayley–Hamilton method leads to an expression that is still tractable:

annotations.note: No 4×4-specific inverse formula via Cayley–Hamilton is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Blockwise inversion formula

anchor.snippet: where A , B , C and D are matrix sub-blocks of arbitrary size

annotations.note: `Matrix.invOf_fromBlocks₂₂_eq` and `invOf_fromBlocks₁₁_eq` give the blockwise inverse via the Schur complement.

annotations.label: Nullity theorem

anchor.snippet: The nullity theorem says that the nullity of A equals the nullity of the sub-block in the lower right of the inverse matrix

annotations.note: The nullity theorem relating nullities of a block and the corresponding block of the inverse is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Woodbury matrix identity

anchor.snippet: where Equation ( 3 ) is the Woodbury matrix identity , which is equivalent to the binomial inverse theorem

annotations.note: `Matrix.add_mul_mul_inv_eq_sub` (and `invOf_add_mul_mul`) is the Woodbury identity, plus `add_mul_mul_inv_eq_sub'` gives the binomial inverse variant.

annotations.label: Weinstein–Aronszajn identity

anchor.snippet: By the Weinstein–Aronszajn identity , one of the two matrices in the block-diagonal matrix is invertible exactly when the other is

annotations.note: `Matrix.det_one_add_mul_comm` is explicitly labelled the Weinstein–Aronszajn identity in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric block inverse formula

anchor.snippet: If the given invertible matrix is a symmetric matrix with invertible block A the following block inverse formula holds

annotations.note: The general blockwise inverse formula covers this case, but no symmetric-block specialization with `C = Bᵀ` is stated.

annotations.label: Neumann series inverse

anchor.snippet: then A is nonsingular and its inverse may be expressed by a Neumann series

annotations.note: No matrix-valued Neumann series inversion result is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neumann series near invertible X

anchor.snippet: More generally, if A is "near" the invertible matrix X in the sense that

annotations.note: No perturbative inverse formula for matrices near an invertible one is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-adic approximation method

anchor.snippet: a p -adic approximation method converges to an exact solution in O( n 4 log 2 n )

annotations.note: No p-adic matrix-inversion algorithm is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reciprocal basis vectors inverse

anchor.snippet: then using Clifford algebra (or geometric algebra ) we compute the reciprocal (sometimes called dual ) column vectors:

annotations.note: No Clifford/geometric-algebra reciprocal-basis construction of the matrix inverse is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Singular matrix

anchor.snippet: Over a field , a square matrix that is not invertible is called singular or degenerate

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `¬ IsUnit A` (equivalently `¬ IsUnit A.det`) for singularity but does not introduce a named `Singular` predicate.

annotations.label: Singular iff determinant zero

anchor.snippet: A square matrix with entries in a field is singular if and only if its determinant is zero

annotations.note: `isUnit_iff_isUnit_det` plus the fact that over a field `IsUnit x ↔ x ≠ 0` yields the singular-iff-zero-determinant characterization.

annotations.label: Invertible matrix theorem

anchor.snippet: The following statements are equivalent, i.e., they are either all true or all false for any given matrix

annotations.note: Many individual equivalences (invertibility, unit determinant, injectivity of `mulVec`, etc.) exist (e.g. `vecMul_surjective_iff_exists_left_inverse`) but no single combined `invertible_matrix_theorem` lemma.

annotations.label: Properties of invertible matrices

anchor.snippet: Furthermore, the following properties hold for an invertible matrix A

annotations.note: Standard properties like `(AB)⁻¹ = B⁻¹A⁻¹` (`mul_inv_rev`), `(A⁻¹)⁻¹ = A` (`inv_inv_of_invertible`), `det A⁻¹ = (det A)⁻¹` (`det_nonsing_inv`), `(Aᵀ)⁻¹ = (A⁻¹)ᵀ` (`transpose_nonsing_inv`) are formalized.

annotations.label: Rows of inverse orthonormal to columns

anchor.snippet: The rows of the inverse matrix V of a matrix U are orthonormal to the columns of U

annotations.note: No statement framing the rows of `A⁻¹` as biorthogonal to the columns of `A` is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Involutory matrix

anchor.snippet: A matrix that is its own inverse (i.e., a matrix A such that A = A −1 and consequently A 2 = I ) is called an involutory matrix

annotations.note: No named `Involutory` predicate for matrices satisfying `A * A = 1` is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inverse via adjugate

anchor.snippet: The adjugate of a matrix A can be used to find the inverse of A as follows:

annotations.note: `Matrix.nonsing_inv_apply` shows `A⁻¹ = (det A)⁻¹ • adjugate A` whenever `det A` is a unit.

annotations.label: One-sided inverse implies two-sided

anchor.section: In relation to the identity matrix
anchor.snippet: It follows from the associativity of matrix multiplication that if

annotations.note: `Matrix.mul_eq_one_comm : A * B = 1 ↔ B * A = 1` makes one-sided inverses two-sided for square matrices.

annotations.label: Singular matrices form a null set

anchor.snippet: the set of singular n -by- n matrices, considered as a subset of

annotations.note: No statement that the singular matrices have Lebesgue measure zero in `Matrix n n ℝ` is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invertible matrices open and dense

anchor.snippet: the set of n -by- n invertible matrices is open and dense in the topological space of all n -by- n matrices

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes continuity of `Matrix.inv` (`Topology.Instances.Matrix`) but does not prove openness/density of `GLₙ` in `Matrix n n ℝ`.

annotations.label: Derivative of matrix inverse

anchor.snippet: Suppose that the invertible matrix A depends on a parameter t . Then the derivative of the inverse of A with respect to t is given by

annotations.note: No formula `d/dt (A⁻¹) = -A⁻¹ (dA/dt) A⁻¹` for the derivative of a matrix-valued inverse is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Left/right inverses of non-square matrices

anchor.snippet: Non-square matrices, i.e. m -by- n matrices for which m ≠ n , do not have an inverse

annotations.note: No statement explicitly addressing the existence/uniqueness of one-sided inverses for non-square matrices is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invertibility over rings

anchor.snippet: in the case of a ring being commutative , the condition for a square matrix to be invertible is that its determinant is invertible in the ring

annotations.note: `isUnit_iff_isUnit_det` proves over a commutative ring that a square matrix is a unit iff its determinant is a unit.

annotations.label: General linear group

anchor.snippet: The set of n × n invertible matrices together with the operation of matrix multiplication and entries from ring R form a group , the general linear group of degree n

annotations.note: `Matrix.GeneralLinearGroup n R := (Matrix n n R)ˣ` formalizes `GLₙ(R)` as the unit group.
